comment.body: the selling was exacerbated by both dealers and institutions, it wasn't just dealer flows (can tell by  the fact we tore right through 5100 towards EOD, where there was a lot of positive gamma). So he's calling a pivot here and we could bounce but i think its going to look more like March 13 - March 31 (just choppy). Even if you get a vol decline its not going to cause a rally because of ITM  puts so at best itll be a modest bounce imo. I don't see anything changing here unless some sort of positive news comes out

Hey @Mario , thanks to all your hints and tips — and of course Alma’s posts — I was able to calculate the sigma levels. But mine are quite different from Alma’s. For the 68.2% level, there’s a 46-point difference. Looks like my predicted volatility is higher.
I used ^GSPC yahoo daily price data for SPX, from 2017-12-31 to 2025-02-24 to fit the model. Does this model result match yours?
Not sure where I might be going wrong.

comment.body: Hey @marrom, I realize I had a typo in my reply to you yesterday about the Fib levels (you asked, custom or standard).  I used several of standard Fibs between 0 and 1 (which I believe are the only Fibs, b/c they are just defined mathematical ratios anyway).  0.5, 0.618, 0.786 (typo was in the last one).  Caveat that our esteemed teacher does not rely upon them in her analysis.  I see she is online and can correct me if I am misstating that.  For my part, I often see algo trading bouncing around them, and also think there is some reflexivity b/c many believe in them so I check them sometimes.
comment.raw_body: Hey ${0}, I realize I had a typo in my reply to you yesterday about the Fib levels (you asked, custom or standard).  I used several of standard Fibs between 0 and 1 (which I believe are the only Fibs, b/c they are just defined mathematical ratios anyway).  0.5, 0.618, 0.786 (typo was in the last one). comment.body:
Hi Alma, coming back to your comment in Friday’s chat: “Then boom, vol squeeze when EU responds; especially if they sell debt – as I expect.”

You’ve mentioned that the EU tariffs come at a time when the EU economy is still grappling with post-pandemic recovery and external shocks, and that these tariffs are being used to pressure the EU into accepting the "Holy Alliance 2.0" peace proposal. With Trump aiming to solve Western stagflation by Easter - and deliberately engineering a mild recession tocreate a negative wealth effect, while carefully avoiding a deflationary spiral - we’ve already seen a drop in the US 10Y yield and significantly falling oil prices. That suggests deflation fears are beginning to creep into investor sentiment, and that everything is currently going according to plan.

I’m trying to understand your thinking around why you expect the EU to strike back by selling U.S. debt. As you pointed out, such a move could depress bond prices, push up interest rates, and potentially destabilize global markets. Do you think they would go this far precisely to disrupt Trump’s strategy (e.g., inducing lower rates, etc.) or do you see this as part of the overall plan?

Specifically the cost of technology must be significantly lower so that arab investors find it an attractive deal (mission almost complete?). After buying tech at lower costs, funds can be allocated into debt instruments and bonds (where a selloff in bonds triggered by the EU creates strong entry points?)
comment.raw_body:
Hi Alma, coming back to your comment in Friday’s chat: “Then boom, vol squeeze when EU responds; especially if they sell debt – as I expect.”

You’ve mentioned that the EU tariffs come at a time when the EU economy is still grappling with post-pandemic recovery and external shocks, and that these tariffs are being used to pressure the EU into accepting the "Holy Alliance 2.0" peace proposal. With Trump aiming to solve Western stagflation by Easter - and deliberately engineering a mild recession tocreate a negative wealth effect, while carefully avoiding a deflationary spiral - we’ve already seen a drop in the US 10Y yield and significantly falling oil prices. That suggests deflation fears are beginning to creep into investor sentiment, and that everything is currently going according to plan.

I’m trying to understand your thinking around why you expect the EU to strike back by selling U.S. debt. As you pointed out, such a move could depress bond prices, push up interest rates, and potentially destabilize global markets. Do you think they would go this far precisely to disrupt Trump’s strategy (e.g., inducing lower rates, etc.) or do you see this as part of the overall plan?

Specifically the cost of technology must be significantly lower so that arab investors find it an attractive deal (mission almost complete?). After buying tech at lower costs, funds can be allocated into debt instruments and bonds (where a selloff in bonds triggered by the EU creates strong entry points?)
